channel.title: Audio on RadixArk Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Audio on RadixArk Blog

item.title: The Fourier Transform in Audio Processing
item.description:
When handling input signals for Speech-to-Text inference, the waveform is practically useless in its raw amplitude-over-time format. We must map it mathematically using Fourier.

Continuous Fourier Transform

The Fourier Transform decomposes a function of time \( f(t) \) into its constituent frequencies:

$$ \hat{f}(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-i 2 \pi \xi t} dt $$

Where:

  • \( \xi \) represents frequency.
  • \( \hat{f}(\xi) \) is the complex representation of the frequency domains.
  • \( e^{-i 2 \pi \xi t} \) is derived from Euler’s formula \( e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x \).

Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)

For digital inputs, we use the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), usually calculated efficiently via the FFT algorithm:
